frames.caption: Sort first: [-1, 0, 1, 2, -1, -4] → [-4, -1, -1, 0, 1, 2]. Sorting is what licenses the converging inner scan and makes duplicate-skipping a simple "equals the neighbor" test.
frames.note: Sorting is the enabling move. It costs O(n log n) — dominated by the O(n²) scan that follows — and in return every inner pair search becomes a two-pointer converge, and duplicates of any value land adjacent so they can be skipped with one comparison.

frames.caption: Anchor i=0 (value -4). Set L=1, R=5 and converge on the suffix for a pair summing to 4.
frames.note: The anchor fixes one of the three values, turning a 3-sum into a 2-sum on the suffix: find a pair in (i, n) summing to −nums[i]. Because the array is sorted, that pair search is a converging two-pointer scan, which is what drops the cost from a brute-force O(n³) triple loop to O(n²) overall.

frames.caption: sum = -4 + -1 + 2 = -3 < 0 → move L right.
frames.note: The triple undershoots 0. On sorted data the only way to grow the sum is a larger left value, so advance L rightward. Every L++ permanently discards nums[L] as a partner for this anchor — that monotone, no-backtrack movement is why each anchor costs only O(n).

frames.caption: sum = -4 + -1 + 2 = -3 < 0 → move L right.
frames.note: The triple undershoots 0. On sorted data the only way to grow the sum is a larger left value, so advance L rightward. Every L++ permanently discards nums[L] as a partner for this anchor — that monotone, no-backtrack movement is why each anchor costs only O(n).

frames.caption: sum = -4 + 0 + 2 = -2 < 0 → move L right.
frames.note: The triple undershoots 0. On sorted data the only way to grow the sum is a larger left value, so advance L rightward. Every L++ permanently discards nums[L] as a partner for this anchor — that monotone, no-backtrack movement is why each anchor costs only O(n).

frames.caption: sum = -4 + 1 + 2 = -1 < 0 → move L right.
frames.note: The triple undershoots 0. On sorted data the only way to grow the sum is a larger left value, so advance L rightward. Every L++ permanently discards nums[L] as a partner for this anchor — that monotone, no-backtrack movement is why each anchor costs only O(n).

frames.caption: L and R crossed (L=5, R=5) — anchor 0 is exhausted. Advance to the next anchor.
frames.note: When L meets or passes R the suffix has no more untested pairs for this anchor, so the inner scan ends. Each of the n anchors runs one such O(n) converge, giving the O(n²) total.

frames.caption: Anchor i=1 (value -1). Set L=2, R=5 and converge on the suffix for a pair summing to 1.
frames.note: The anchor fixes one of the three values, turning a 3-sum into a 2-sum on the suffix: find a pair in (i, n) summing to −nums[i]. Because the array is sorted, that pair search is a converging two-pointer scan, which is what drops the cost from a brute-force O(n³) triple loop to O(n²) overall.

frames.caption: sum = -1 + -1 + 2 = 0 → record [-1, -1, 2], then move both pointers inward and skip duplicates.
frames.note: sum == 0: record the triplet, then move BOTH pointers inward. Moving only one would re-test the same value against a guaranteed non-match; advancing both is also what prevents an infinite loop. The skip-duplicate while-loops then jump L and R past any repeats so the identical triplet is never emitted twice.

frames.caption: sum = -1 + 0 + 1 = 0 → record [-1, 0, 1], then move both pointers inward and skip duplicates.
frames.note: sum == 0: record the triplet, then move BOTH pointers inward. Moving only one would re-test the same value against a guaranteed non-match; advancing both is also what prevents an infinite loop. The skip-duplicate while-loops then jump L and R past any repeats so the identical triplet is never emitted twice.

frames.caption: L and R crossed (L=4, R=3) — anchor 1 is exhausted. Advance to the next anchor.
frames.note: When L meets or passes R the suffix has no more untested pairs for this anchor, so the inner scan ends. Each of the n anchors runs one such O(n) converge, giving the O(n²) total.

frames.caption: i=2: nums[i]=-1 equals nums[1]=-1 — duplicate anchor, skip to avoid repeating triplets.
frames.note: This anchor equals the previous one, so every triplet it could form was already found when nums[i-1] was the anchor. Skipping it is exactly what keeps the result set unique without a post-hoc dedupe pass.

frames.caption: Anchor i=3 (value 0). Set L=4, R=5 and converge on the suffix for a pair summing to 0.
frames.note: The anchor fixes one of the three values, turning a 3-sum into a 2-sum on the suffix: find a pair in (i, n) summing to −nums[i]. Because the array is sorted, that pair search is a converging two-pointer scan, which is what drops the cost from a brute-force O(n³) triple loop to O(n²) overall.

frames.caption: sum = 0 + 1 + 2 = 3 > 0 → move R left.
frames.note: The triple overshoots 0. The only way to shrink it is a smaller right value, so retreat R leftward. nums[R] paired with the smallest available left already overshot, so it overshoots with every remaining left too — its whole column is retired.

frames.caption: L and R crossed (L=4, R=4) — anchor 3 is exhausted. Advance to the next anchor.
frames.note: When L meets or passes R the suffix has no more untested pairs for this anchor, so the inner scan ends. Each of the n anchors runs one such O(n) converge, giving the O(n²) total.

frames.caption: Done. All unique triplets summing to 0: [-1, -1, 2], [-1, 0, 1].
frames.note: Every anchor has been processed and the duplicate-skips kept the set unique. The whole thing is O(n²) time — an O(n log n) sort plus an O(n) converge per anchor — and O(1) auxiliary space beyond the output list.
